sentences.text: Interview with Agnes Vertes.
Maiden name Katz.
Today is January 31, 1996.
My name is Michelle [?
Landerberger. ?] We're conducting this interview in Weston, Connecticut, and the interview language is English.
I was telling you about my first encounter with the treatment of the Jews by the Nazis.
And it had a tremendous impact on me.
This has been haunting me in my dreams and all through my lifetime.
And I don't know if it's appropriate to mention, but when I saw and they're herding the people through the streets of Krakow, I was hysterical in the movie theater.
I was absolutely hysterical.
My daughter, whom I took there, she didn't shed a tear, but I-- the whole thing came back.
So after that, we went to my mother's hiding place.
And my father came there too.
He risked coming out of his hiding place and he came to see us.
No sooner did we all arrive there, there was an air raid.

And of course, my mother thought we shouldn't go down to the shelter because we all had our story and my mother's story was that her husband had left for the front and, well, did not abandon her, but she had no way of supporting her two kids and therefore we were given to somebody else.
Our story was coinciding with it, but not quite.
Our story was that our father, Andrew Kovacs, was fighting the German side on the front and we had to be placed because our mother is a refugee from Transylvania.
Because by that time the Russian's were encroaching on Transylvania, and so a lot of Hungarian-speaking people came back to Hungary from Transylvania.
Anyway, the sirens started, and to me, that gave me the chills, the sirens.
They still do.
I turned literally green.
I was so afraid.
I couldn't stay in one place.
I was shaking.
And my sister, however, she was two years old, and she just learn to speak.
And she had a bunting bag, and every time there was an air raid, she had to be in the bunting bag, and then she was safe.
So she was yelling, quick, quick!
Get me the bag, the bag, the bag!
And once they put her in the bag, my mother zipped her up, she says to me, don't worry, Agica, nothing is going to happen.
The plane won't thrown bomb.
The plane won't throw bomb.
No bomb from plane.
And she was happy as a clam sitting in her bunting bag.
But I was deathly pale, and my father decided that he wasn't going to take me down to the basement of the apartment building to the shelter, but he would take me across the street.
And across the street was a school.
And the principal of the school was a big Nazi, Hungarian Nazi, and we went in there and the principal was making all sorts of speeches.
And I'm sure that my father was thinking, oh my God, if anybody recognizes us or somebody comes in and asks for papers.
All they have to do is ask him to drop his pants.
Nobody was circumcised unless they were Jews.
But fortunately my great fortune was that I did not look Jewish.
Nobody thought I was Jewish.
And somehow we got away with it.
The air raid ended, my father took me back upstairs, and he went home to his place-- home, whenever he was hiding.
And then every day my mother and Uncle Rega took us to different places trying to place us.
Because it was dangerous to be with her.
And I remember that one day we went to some place near the area where we used to live to look for a home for children.
And nobody wanted to take us and my mother was kind of discouraged.
And we're going home on the tram, and all of a sudden there was another air raid.
And this time, she didn't want to go down to any shelter because she was worried that somebody might recognize her or us at that point.
Because that was the district we used to live in.
So we went to hide under the entrance of an apartment building.
And all hell broke loose.
The bombs were dropping.
It was just total pandemonium.
It was so noisy, and you could hear the buildings collapsed and the terrible siren sounds of the bomb.
It's not a siren.
It's a sharp sound.
And people screaming and people being blown to pieces.
It was a horrible, horrible sight.
We were exposed to that too as Jews, in addition to being persecuted, we were exposed to the same dangers as the general population.
So it was multiplied by many-fold.
And when the air raid was over, the street car that we were on was totally demolished.
People were all over the street, dead, and parts of people.
It was a horrendous scene.
After that, I don't know how long it took because time to a child is immaterial, but they did place us.
They found a place for us on the other side of the Danube.
And they took us there.
My mother found place and I believe Uncle Rega took us.
I don't think my mother came with us.
I don't think she risked going with him.
And this was a very nice place, unlike where we were before.
This happened to have been the mansion of the mayor of Buda, or the former mayor of Buda.
Of course, he wasn't living there anymore.
There were over 100 kids living in it.
It had a gorgeous park around it, gorgeous park.
And while it wasn't too cold we could go out there and play and have fun.
It was nice.
And as I said, we were 100 kids there, and a number of adults who were taking care of us.
Now later I found out that all 100 of us were Jewish kids, but he thought that were the genuine article.
So things were kind of OK there.
But every once in a while, a number of times a week, every week, the Nazis would come in to raid the place.
Papers, papers, everybody's papers.
And the two of us were supposed to have been the only authentic 'Aryans.' And each time the Nazis came they pushed my sister and myself in front and aren't they cute?
And somehow we got away with it.
And then one day a whole bunch of them came in and the women were desperate.
They thought that this is it, this is it.
And my sister, who was really cute, I mean she was-- this is 1944, maybe November or so-- she was about two years old at the time.
She runs over to one of them and she says to him, hey, mister soldier, show me your cap.
So the soldier picks her up and throws her In the air.
And he looks at his companions and he says, only an Aryan child could be as cute as this one.
And the women were so happy and they gave them drinks and they left, the Nazis, and they left us alone.
So things were kind of OK, and Christmas came.
And they had a huge Christmas tree.
Because, of course, this was supposed to have been a Christian home for children.
And we had fun decorating the Christmas tree.
And everybody depended on me because they thought I was the real thing that I will know all the prayers and songs and everything.
And, indeed, I did.
I've never seen a Christmas tree before in my life.
We didn't use to have a Christmas tree at home.
We used to light Hanukkah candles.
So Christmas was kind of OK.
But after that, really the war came home to us because now this is January and the Russians are encircling the city.
And, in fact, in mid-January Pest side was already free.
This side was not.
Buda was not.
The Nazis-- Germans and Hungarians together-- took up positions on the roof of the mansion.
The Russians were at the end of the park at the gates.
It had high fences, stone fences, fancy fences.
And the Russians were down at the stone fences, the Germans on top of the roof.
And they were shooting at one another and we were down in the house.

So in order to keep us from getting shot from stray bullets, the adults pushed all of the cupboards and like the secretary, things like that, wardrobes, against all the windows that we're facing that side of the house.

And the largest room downstairs, where we used to eat and where we had Christmas tree before, was totally emptied of furniture and everybody brought down their mattress and we were lying down on the floor with the mattresses on top of us so that if any of the bullets came through, it first had to go through a piece of furniture and then through the mattress.
And my sister and I were under one mattress, and we were clutching one another.
And I was always afraid.
I am such a chicken-hearted person.
I was always afraid.
My sister, she was too little to understand.
So this went on for many days, and we children were only allowed to go to the toilet.
Other than that, we were on the floor, under the mattress.
And of course nobody could go out shopping or anything, so we were running very low on food.
And by a certain time we only had flour left.
And at some point the women looked outside and she saw that the house was on fire.
And there we are, 100 kids inside the house.
No sooner does she tell us, everybody up, the Nazis from to roof come downstairs and they are starved.
They are starved like animals.
And they tell the adults that nobody leaves here until we get food.
And we didn't have any.
So finally somehow one of the women negotiated with them that she will make from flour and water pancakes, but they have to let us go.
And by that time the house was pretty much gone.
The construction is different there.
It was stone.
It wasn't wood, but it was a horrible time.
To this day I'm claustrophobic because of being trapped in a burning house.
And finally we were told we could go.
And I remember I put on as much clothing as possible.
They dressed my sister because she was a baby.
But I dressed so funny.
I had on top a knit outfit, matching pants and top, and the hood and the gloves.
And on top of it I put my robe.
This I remember.
Isn't that stupid you remember 50 years back you dressed so stupid.
But I thought it was a warm robe and it will help me.
So it was night time and it was very, very cold.
It was a very cold winter.
And I was fortunate, because had it not be so cold I think that half the population would have died of some sort of sickness.
Anyway, I had to walk because I was a big girl.
Susan was carried because she could barely walk.
And it was so cold and so much snow and ice that I kept falling.
And it was so terribly cold that there were body's frozen into the street that we were walking in, bodies and body parts frozen into the ice.
And because don't forget the fighting has been going on between the Russians and the Germans now for some time.
And nobody cleaned up the street.
Anyone who was killed was on the street.
And fortunately it was cold, so they were frozen into the ground.
And I was stumbling, and I remember I stumbled over this body with the eyes open and I was so scared.
I was so scared, I couldn't even scream.
It was such a horrible thing.
And I kept stumbling after that.
This was my worst experience during the Holocaust, the absolute worst.
Because being a little four-year-old and stumbling over these dead bodies, it's just, I can't explain it.
It was horrifying, absolutely horrifying.
And we didn't know where to go.
We finally arrived a barn that belong to somebody's estate.
But the estate was in shambles, in ruins.
The barn was standing and there was a lot of hay in there, so we slept there with the horses.
No food, but at least we had a place to sleep.
And no heat.
It was just everybody slept in their clothing in the hay.
Then the next morning they decided that we have to split up to many groups because nobody's going to take in 100 children.
So my group was the youngest children.
And we went with two adults.
And I don't know where those two adults found two sleds.
And they were pulling us on a sled.
And we hardly ever met anybody on the street.
There were only dead people on the street.
Nobody went outside because there was fighting going on all around.
And whenever they could, they pulled us on the sled, but except for Susie, my sister, we had to take turns walking because it was kind of heavy for the women.
And the next day I remember that evening was coming and we had no place to stay.
And we found a bombed-out house, an apartment building, but there was one part of it that was intact and even had working stove, a kitchen.
Now mind you, not electric stove like I have.
A wood-burning stove on which you could cook.
And there was no water and no food.
And we haven't eaten in some time now.
And one of the women found among the rubbish some wood, made a fire, and we kids went outside.
We found a pot.
We went outside and we collected snow, and we put it on the fire, melted it, boiled it, and I tell you, water never tasted so good.
We were hungry and thirsty, but there was nothing to eat, so we just drank water.
And we went to sleep on the floor in the kitchen because that's where the stove was.
But of course the stove went out and we were frozen by the time we woke up the next morning.
And we started on our way again in the morning.
And I don't remember where we slept again, but I assume in another abandoned building.
And then the next day after that they were pulling us on this sleds, and it became very dark and it started snowing.
And we were just freezing, absolutely freezing, and I must have taken my duster or whatever off, and I was traveling light in my knit snow suit.
And very hungry and very tired and thirsty and miserable and cold.
All of a sudden, I saw somebody walking towards us.
And we never met anybody on the street.
There just simply weren't any people walking around.
Wasn't that safe.
And I thought I was hallucinating.
The figure it came closer and closer.
You know how through the snowflakes the picture is kind of fuzzy and then you have to be real close.
And the woman started running, and all of sudden, I look at her, and it was my mother.
It was my mother.
And she picked us both up and she carried us.
And it was like a miracle, it was like a miracle.
And that night we arrived at a place that was provided by a Protestant pastor's home.
And that was-- his name was Stelo.
The pastor's name is Stelo.
And he was another righteous Christian.
He was helping Jewish boys live through this utter madness by providing them with food and everything in his house and also manufacturing forged papers for them.
And hiding Jews was very dangerous to the person who committed such a crime.
And not only was it punishable by prison, maybe death, but it was more difficult to hide boys because of the circumcision problem.
Anyway, we arrived there and he said he had a full house, but because it's a snowstorm we could spend the night.
But he has no cots or anything, no beds.
We'd have to sleep on the floor.
And we said that's fine.
They had very little food.
I remember that we got three or four stalks of asparagus, canned asparagus, for dinner.
And then again for breakfast.
As long as I was there it was asparagus, and I never wanted to eat asparagus again until about 10 years ago.
Anyway, so that night we spent in a room, sleeping on the floor.
I couldn't sleep all night because my mother was there and I was talking to her all night.
And she told me that she came across the Danube.
There were no bridges.
The bridges were all-- well, they blew them up so that the Russians can't come across that way.
The Nazi blew the bridges up.
And so my mother already was liberated.
This is after Pest was already liberated, but Buda was not yet liberated.
The Russians had not won that side of the city yet.
And when she was liberated, the first thing she wanted to do was to find us.
So she and my Aunt Elsa set out to find us.
When they got to the Danube and there were no bridges they tried to find a way of getting across.
And having been a very cold winter, they thought maybe they could walk across, but some parts of the Danube, well, it's a river and some parts of it were frozen and some were not.
And my mother and my aunt arrived there and the Russians are on the bank of the Danube band and screaming at them that they want workers, workers.
And my aunt said to my mother, look, you go across.
You find the children.
I'll keep them at bay here.
I'll go work.
And so my aunt started sweeping snow or whatever she had to do, and my mother gave some money to some Russian soldier to row her across, two Russian soldiers.
And this I know from later.
She told me that night, but I had forgotten, and then she again told me about it.
So she got to a certain point with the two Russians, and she paid them with a gold watch.
They were crazy about watches, the Russians, as you know.
And they used to go around collect everybody's watches and they had watches up to here on their arms.
And somehow they got caught in an undercurrent or I don't know what, some sort of current.
And one of the Russians fell out of the boat and disappeared.
The other Russian got so mad, put my mother out on the island in the middle of the river, abandoned her there.
And coming from the island to the Buda side, I don't know how my mother got there, but I think she told me that she was hopping from ice to ice.
And then she went to the Red Cross to try to see if they know where we were.
And the Red Cross told her that the house where we were burned down, but that we were alive but they don't know where we are.
And she just set out to go from home to home.
And the second day she got caught in this snowstorm.
And she was walking, and she was very, very tired and she thought that she saw an apparition.
She thought she was these two sleds, and then she recognized the knitted outfit that she knitted for me.
And she still thought that she was imagining things, and that's when she started running.
And she came in she saw that it was us.
But anyway, all night I was talking to her and I was telling her all the things that bothered me and all the things that happened.
And I said to her, you know, Mom, I dreamt last night that Mama C-- that's what I called my grandmother on my father's side.
And I was closer to her because she lived nearer-- that Mama C had a bloody head and she was on the floor and she was yelling and nobody helped her.
And my mother said, don't be silly.
Mama C is in the sanatorium, and I'm going to go see her tomorrow.
Your grandmothers are fine.
So the next morning, after a breakfast of asparagus, my mother left.
She couldn't take us with her because she didn't want to expose us to the dangers of crossing a semi-frozen river without a bridge.
And she didn't want to stay because she wanted to see how her mother and everybody else was.
I don't know if she ever got there to see my grandparents, because this is the irony of faith.
Around Christmas time, my cousin Peter, who was nine years old during the Holocaust, was very bored with all those adults.
And so he and his mother came in to visit my mother.
And in the meantime they blew up the bridges and they couldn't get back.
Now on January 14-- This was Christmas.
They couldn't get back.
On January 14, somebody had denounced the Jews at the sanatorium to the Nazis.
The Nazis came, dragged everybody out of bed then whoever wasn't in bed.
They tortured them.
They shot them.
And then they poured gasoline over them and they burnt them.
So after the war my father and my uncle went with the dental charts to identify the remains of the family.
So my mother almost denounced my grandmother to get her mother there from the ghetto.
In the ghetto, my aunt survived with the two cousins.
My grandmother died in [INAUDIBLE].
My cousin and my aunt came just for a visit, got stuck there, couldn't go back.
They survive.
So serendipity.
Couldn't be too smart.
